question: When does TypeScript NOT check excess properties?

options.text: When assigning through an intermediate variable (indirect assignment)
When directly assigning an object literal
options.explanation: Direct assignment is precisely the case where TypeScript checks excess properties and reports an error

options.text: When using interfaces instead of type aliases
options.explanation: Excess property checking doesn't depend on interfaces vs type aliases. It depends on the assignment method (direct vs indirect)

options.text: When using strict mode
options.explanation: Strict mode doesn't affect excess property checks. This check works the same in strict and non-strict modes

question: How does the Without<T, U> type work in the XOR implementation?

options.text: Makes keys from T that are absent in U optional with type never
Removes all keys of T from U
options.explanation: Without doesn't remove keys, it makes 'foreign' keys optional with type never. This allows TypeScript to forbid their presence in the object

options.text: Creates an intersection T & U
options.explanation: Without creates a type with keys from Exclude<keyof T, keyof U>, each with type never. This is the opposite of an intersection

options.text: Returns never if T and U overlap
options.explanation: Without doesn't return never — it creates a mapped type with optional never properties, which forbids their presence in the object

question: Which statements are true for branded (opaque) types?

options.text: Brand<number, 'UserId'> is incompatible with Brand<number, 'PostId'>
A branded type cannot be assigned from a plain number without an as-assertion
Branded types add runtime overhead
options.explanation: Branded types exist only at the type level. In compiled JavaScript the brand is completely erased — it's a zero-cost abstraction

options.text: A smart constructor is required to create a branded type
options.explanation: Technically you can use as-assertion directly. But smart constructors are a best practice since they add runtime validation and a single point of creation

question: What does DeepPick<User, 'profile.settings.theme'> produce for User = { id: number; profile: { settings: { theme: string; language: string } } }?

options.text: { profile: { settings: { theme: string } } } — nested structure with one field at each level
{ theme: string } — only the leaf value
options.explanation: DeepPick preserves the entire nested path structure. The result is not a flat object but the full hierarchy from root to target property

options.text: string — the type of the theme property itself
options.explanation: DeepPick returns an object type preserving the hierarchy, not the leaf value type. Getting the value type requires a different approach

options.text: Pick<User, 'profile'> — regular Pick at the top level
options.explanation: DeepPick is not equivalent to Pick. Pick<User, 'profile'> returns the entire profile with all nested properties, while DeepPick returns only the path to theme

question: Why is UnionToIntersection needed for DeepPick with multiple paths?

options.text: Because DeepPick for union paths creates a union of results, but an intersection is needed to access all fields
Because TypeScript doesn't support unions of string literals
options.explanation: TypeScript supports unions of string literals perfectly. The issue is that distributive conditional types turn union paths into union results, not intersections

options.text: Because without intersection there would be a recursion error
options.explanation: Recursion works the same with union or intersection. The problem is semantic: union of results only allows access to common fields, while intersection allows access to all

options.text: Because it's a compilation performance optimization
options.explanation: UnionToIntersection is not an optimization but a necessity. Without it, DeepPick<User, 'id' | 'profile.avatar'> returns {id: number} | {profile: {avatar: string}} — you can't access both fields
